Shenzhen is located in China's Pearl River Delta (PRD) urban agglomeration, a region where rapid urbanization and industrialization have led to serious, complex atmospheric pollution (Sin et al., 2001; He et al., 2011). With the implementation of strict environmental protection measures in recent years, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in Shenzhen has decreased significantly. From 2013 to 2019, the average value of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Shenzhen was reduced from 40 to  $24~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3$  (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). However, due to the high temperatures and high solar radiation characteristics of this region, atmospheric photochemical reactions are active, and the concentration of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  was increasing at an average annual rate of 0.7 ppb from 2014 to 2018 (Wang et al., 2020), such that  $\mathrm{O_3}$  is replacing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as the primary atmospheric pollutant in the region (Huang et al., 2019). Several previous short-term studies have observed the diurnal and seasonal patterns of OVOCs and identified their sources in this region (Liu et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2012; Louie et al., 2013; Han et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019); however, this is the first long-term study of OVOCs that permits the observation of interannual variability and long-term changes, in particular those related to emission reduction measures.

metadata.title: Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.
references: A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated

In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\sim 73\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\sim 35\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.

The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may

be due to the dilution caused by the uplift of the planetary boundary layer (PBL). However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.

The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

metadata.title: Fig. 3. (a) The 24-h backward trajectories calculated for 12:00 every day (local time) at an arrival altitude of  100m  above ground level in Shenzhen during the sampling periods in 2016; (b) ratio of the concentration of OVOCs in three types of air mass to that in the marine air mass.
references: Using the HYSPLIT modeling system, we calculated the 24-h backward trajectory for 12:00 every day during 2016 at an arrival altitude of  $100\mathrm{m}$  above the ground in Shenzhen. Then, the air mass trajectories were clustered according to the similarity of their spatial distribution to study the difference in the composition and concentration of VOCs under different air mass sources. The clustering principles and processes are described in the user guide of the software (Draxler et al., 2009). As shown in Fig. 3(a), the air

mass was clustered into three types: continental (52%), near coastal (26%), and marine (21%). Fig. 3(b) shows the ratio of the concentration of OVOCs in continental and near coastal air masses compared to the marine air mass. The concentrations of OVOCs in the air masses from continental and near coastal sources were much higher than those in the marine air mass, indicating that anthropogenic activity-influenced air masses carry more pollutants. Considering each OVOC species, the concentrations of acetaldehyde and formic acid in the continental air mass were approximately 1.5 times those in the marine air mass, whereas concentrations of the other four OVOCs were more than 2.5 times those in the marine air mass, indicating that these four OVOCs, with an atmospheric lifetime of more than several days (Atkinson, 2000), were more affected by anthropogenic emissions through regional transport. In addition, the difference between acetaldehyde and formic acid in the three air masses was the smallest, possibly because they were mainly derived from photochemical generation and were not significantly affected by direct anthropogenic emissions.

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\sim 73\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\sim 35\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.

These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.
Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.

PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

references: concentration to obtain OFP (ppbv). Fig. 5 shows the average concentration and OFP of each VOC in the dry and wet seasons over six years. The top three species contributing to OFP in the dry and wet seasons were acetaldehyde, methanol and toluene, which accounted for  $83\%$  and  $78\%$  of the OFP contributed by all species in the dry and wet seasons, respectively. Methanol made a significant contribution to OFP because its atmospheric concentration was the highest among all VOCs, while acetaldehyde and toluene made significant contributions because of their high reactivity. In addition, the contribution of isoprene to OFP was not negligible and was significantly higher in the wet season than in the dry season, which also reflects the increased contribution of biogenic sources in the wet season. Compared with the study of the VOC emission inventory in the PRD (Zheng et al., 2009; Lyu et al., 2020), the contribution of OVOCs in this study was more significant because the emission inventory study usually evaluates only the primary emission source, while the secondary source of OVOCs, such as acetaldehyde, cannot be ignored. Aldehydes not only have high reactivity but also can generate OH radicals by photolysis (Volkamer et al., 2010; Xue et al., 2016). Therefore, aldehydes are important products and intermediates of photochemistry reactions, and they are also the key VOC species for subsequent research on atmospheric oxidation capacity.

To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). metadata.title: Fig. 6. Six-year trends of the contributions from different sources to daytime OVOC concentrations.
references: Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

the OVOC concentrations in 2014). From 2014 to 2019, the ambient concentration of OVOCs contributed by primary anthropogenic sources decreased significantly, declining by  $50 - 60\%$ . Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China.

Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

Generally, CO (de Gouw et al., 2005, 2018), acetylene (Yuan et al., 2012) and benzene (Ma et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019) are commonly used tracers representing anthropogenic sources of VOCs. Anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China are rather complicated, with industrial solvents and motor vehicle exhaust contributing significantly to VOCs. However, CO and acetylene mainly come from combustion emissions, which cannot represent well the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China. Compared with CO and acetylene, benzene can better reflect the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in the PRD. For example, Zhang et al. (2013) used the PMF model to analyze the source of VOCs in the PRD and found that combustion processes and industrial emissions were the predominant sources of benzene. Second, considering the photolysis of aldehydes and ketones during the daytime, which obviously conflicts with assumption (2), we need to consider the effect of the photodegradation of aldehydes and ketones on their removal. Referring to the studies of Wang et al. (2017) and Huang et al. (2020), we introduced a coefficient  $(\mathrm{m})$  before the OH reaction rate constant  $\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{ovoc}}\right)$  to compensate for the daytime loss of aldehydes and ketones caused by photolysis. A detailed description of the calculation method of the m value is given in the supporting information. For methanol, formic acid and acetic acid, with almost no photolysis (Saunders et al., 2003), this study ignored the effect of photolysis on daytime removal (set m as 1), while the calculated m values for acetaldehyde, acetone and methyl ethyl ketone (MEK) were 1.02, 1.26 and 1.25, respectively, during the sampling period. Third, previous studies have shown that biomass burning emissions are an inevitable source of OVOCs (Yuan et al., 2010; Warneke et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2016). For example, Yuan et al. (2010) reported that biomass burning emissions in the PRD contributed  $9.5 - 17.7\%$  to nine kinds of VOCs (including methanol, acetone, and MEK), suggesting that the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs cannot be ignored. Therefore, the last parameter  $(ER_{bb} * [\text{Tracer}_{bb}])$  in Equation (1) was added in this study to estimate the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs.  $[\text{Tracer}_{bb}]$  represents the concentration of ambient acetonitrile, which is an excellent indicator for biomass burning and can be accurately measured by PTR-MS (de Gouw et al., 2003b). In addition, previous studies have shown that benzene mainly comes from transportation and solvent use in the PRD, and biomass burning accounts for less than  $10\%$  (Lyu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013), so the influence of using benzene as a tracer of anthropogenic sources on biomass burning emissions can be ignored.  $\mathrm{ER}_{bb}$  is the emission factor of OVOCs relative to acetonitrile, which was calculated by least-squares fit along with  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{OVOC}}$ ,  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{precursor}}$ ,  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{biogen}}$ ,  $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{precursor}}$ , and [background].

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\%)$

Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.

VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic

PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

references: (Fig. 1 [5,6]). In 2017, approximately  $70\%$  of all 338 municipal cities in China still did not meet the National Ambient Air Quality Standard (NAAQS) [7], with non-attainment of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as the most prominent air pollution problem. China faces the dual pressures of the improvement of national air quality and the mitigation of climate change.

On the other hand, regulations on  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions in China have lagged in comparison with actions on  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ . The sources for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are distributed across multiple sectors involving high-temperature combustions, resulting in increased difficulty and higher costs for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission mitigation. In addition to coal combustion processes, including thermal power and industrial plants, on-road vehicles and other fossil fuel combustion processes are important sources of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions. Total  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions increased threefold during 1990-2010 (Fig. 1), as indicated by both bottom-up estimation and satellite observations [24]. Many large cities have experienced rapid motorization since 2000, resulting in their urban nitrogen dioxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_2)$  concentrations exceeding NAAQS. Increasing trends in the ratios of nitrate  $(\mathrm{NO}_3^-)$  to sulfate  $(\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-})$  in both ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  mass [25] and precipitation [10] have been observed in many regions of China. However, national mandates on controlling  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions were not established until the 12th FYP (2011-2015).

references: A significant negative correlation (Pearson's  $r = -0.90$ ) can be seen between the national average precipitation acidity (i.e., average pH values) and total  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions over the past 20 years (Fig. 2 [10]). The trough of average precipitation pH values and the peak of total  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions both took place in 2006, when about one third of China's total territory area was at risk of acid rain. In terms of bulk sulfur and nitrogen deposition, the severity of the acid rain in China in 2006, when acid rain was at its peak, was estimated in the same range (if not higher) as that in central Europe in the 1980s [8].

Although installation of FGD systems was required by the 2000 amendment of the Air Pollution Law, the implementation was not well enforced [20]. Consequently, the total  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions increased by nearly  $50\%$  during 2002-2006, driven by a surge in coal consumption (Fig. 2), which worsened acid rain pollution nationwide [10]. This situation was improved during the 11th Five-Year Plan (FYP) (2006-2010), which set a concrete target to mitigate total  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions by  $10\%$ . The 11th FYP was considered a milestone during which the air pollution control target became mandatory for provincial and municipal governments for the first time, enforced by the fact that local leaders could receive a sanction in cadre evaluation if they failed [21].

Fig. 4 summarizes the major action plans for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control implemented by the Chinese government since 2013. The policy framework shows a clear trend of gradually involving more categories of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  precursors and emission sectors, since traditional control actions for a single pollutant or a single sector were insufficient to deliver substantial reductions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations as required by the Action Plan. One notable case is that the estimated  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions and sulfate concentrations inversed from satellite observations both rebounded slightly during 2010-2011 due to a rapid increase in industrial activities and less stringent controls on the iron and steel industries (Fig. 2), which surpassed the reduction benefits of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  controls on power plants [31]. As a result, a series of stringent control measures have been implemented in the industrial polluting sector, including strengthening industrial emissions standards, phasing out small-scale high-polluting factories, phasing out outdated industrial capacities, and upgrading industrial boilers [32]. references: $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution (Fig. 3). Control actions on acid rain were primarily focused on  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions from coal combustion in the 1990s. However, the increasing trend of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions was not reversed until 2007, when diversified technological and policy instruments were widely utilized. Such progress could not have been achieved without innovative improvement in political accountability, verification of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and financial incentives for power plants to install and operate flue gas desulfurization (FGD).  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  has more sources than  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , including coal burning, mobile sources, and other fossil fuel combustion sectors. Therefore, control actions on  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions must be more extensive and complex, which requires cooperation among multiple sectors. Controlling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations not only requires consistently mitigating multiple precursors from numerous sectors, but also demands consideration of complex influences from both meteorological and atmospheric chemistry conditions. Indeed, comprehensive control actions with multi-party coordination on provincial and even national levels have been implemented in China to minimize the adverse ecological and social impacts of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. Since 2013, combating  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution has marked a strategic transfer from emission control toward air quality management in China, which is oriented toward benefiting the public health and social welfare. The fundamental improvement of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations has become a key indicator of the success of ecological civilization construction in China.

As Fig. 4 indicates, control actions have been implemented nationwide, with additional efforts in several key regions. Improving air quality and mitigating  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in core megacities (e.g., Beijing and Shanghai) have been attained due to increased attention from the central government and the public. Beijing provides a success story in tackling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution after two-decade-long efforts. In fact, the implementation of comprehensive air pollution controls in Beijing was initiated as early as the 1990s. During the 2008 Olympic Games, extensive control measures were implemented in Beijing and surrounding provinces to guarantee good air quality [41]. Ambient concentrations of CO,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  continuously decreased, even as the municipal GDP, resident population, vehicle stock, and energy consumption increased significantly from 1998 to 2013. references: Guided by the Action Plan, many other cities in China are following Beijing to reduce ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 5). The strategic promotion of ecological civilization has spurred stronger willingness, clearer targets, more supportive legislation and incentives, and more stringent implementation of control actions from central to local governments. All these factors work together with public engagement to achieve progress in controlling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and improving public health and welfare. During the Action Plan 2013-2017,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations deceased by  $23\%$  on average for all the municipal cities in China [46] (Fig. 5). Reductions for the three key regions were even more significant:  $40\%$  for BTH,  $34\%$  for YRD, and  $28\%$  for PRD. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in the PRD region was  $34~\mu \mathrm{g}\cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which adhered to NAAQS for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . This progress has motivated some local governments to actively target more stringent limits on  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$

references: Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\mathrm{h}$ $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\%$  and  $24.5\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\% -29\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.

The biggest challenge of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy.

references: considerably decreased from 2013 to 2020. As shown in Fig. 6, the AQI of all studied cities was all less than 100 since 2017, which is the threshold AQI value of excessive pollutants, indicating that air quality in China began to pick up. Herein, for better comparison, the 31 provincial capital cities were categorized into 7 districts according to the official administrative geographical area division. An identical trend was seen to that in central China, which showed the greatest improvement in air quality during 7 years.

Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.

The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\%$  as the validation set and  $20\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).

During the period of CCTPs implementation, an obvious air quality improvement was observed in the Chinese mainland, manifested as a decrease of AQI value by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020. It is concluded that the clean and efficient transformation of coal resources conforms to China's national conditions, and could indeed improve the atmospheric environment, which has been demonstrated in the existing research as well (Li and Patino-Echeverri, 2017; Liu et al., 2021). Specifically, correlation coefficients showed that the promotion of emission reduction and deindustrialization could fundamentally help to reduce airborne contamination, although their persistence relies on continued efforts and is influenced by various factors (Yang and Teng, 2018). Another important observation was a 2-year time lag before the stringent CCTPs starting from 2015 takes any strong positive effects, shown as a dramatical reduction of AQI in 2018. It implies that the influence of time lag should be considered in the decision-making process, as a time lag commonly appears in air pollution regulations and plans (Van der et al., 2017).

The increase in population size and economic activity has remarkably increased the demand for fossil fuels in China, posing a severe threat to the environment. However, the recently implemented coal-based energy transition has shifted the energy structure from polluting energies to environment-friendly energies, and the subsequent policies created a great revolution in energy development and environment protection. Nevertheless, the energy distribution and air pollution in China are characterized by regionality, thus, targeting the efficiency of these national control policies, scholars mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries, such as Shanxi province and the BJH region; or target a specific policy, such as CTG and CTE policy. Differently, this study revealed the air quality improvement during the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Clarifying the internal mechanism of these policies is significant when revising and implementing the following policies to achieve sustainable development. The quantitative assessment provided significant results: i) After implementing CCTPs, the Chinese mainland experienced air quality improvement, with the reduction of AQI averaging  $18.82\%$ ; ii) An identical reduction of air pollution was observed in 2018, indicating a 2-year time lag of the related control policies; iii) There were positive associations between emission reduction promoting, deindustrialization and air quality improvement, suggesting two promising ways to realize sustainable energy development. Through assessing the air quality changes before and after the implementation of CCTPs, it is expected to provide a scientific basis for policymakers and stakeholders to forge consensus on the establishment and enforcement of clean coal policies.

However, the increasing trend of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions was not reversed until 2007, when diversified technological and policy instruments were widely utilized. Such progress could not have been achieved without innovative improvement in political accountability, verification of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and financial incentives for power plants to install and operate flue gas desulfurization (FGD).  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  has more sources than  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , including coal burning, mobile sources, and other fossil fuel combustion sectors. Therefore, control actions on  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions must be more extensive and complex, which requires cooperation among multiple sectors. Controlling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations not only requires consistently mitigating multiple precursors from numerous sectors, but also demands consideration of complex influences from both meteorological and atmospheric chemistry conditions. Indeed, comprehensive control actions with multi-party coordination on provincial and even national levels have been implemented in China to minimize the adverse ecological and social impacts of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. Since 2013, combating  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution has marked a strategic transfer from emission control toward air quality management in China, which is oriented toward benefiting the public health and social welfare. The fundamental improvement of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations has become a key indicator of the success of ecological civilization construction in China.

A tunnel in the city center of Shanghai  $(31.24^{\circ}\mathrm{N}, 121.50^{\circ}\mathrm{E})$  was selected to conduct air pollution monitoring. The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is  $2,107\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is  $33.75\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at  $150\mathrm{m}$  away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about  $1.2\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in each unit. NO,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were  $0.01\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $0.01\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and  $0.15\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was  $0.1\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network.
